Hypothesis: Changing the hero CTA from “Shop Now” to “Get 20% Off Your First Order” will increase add-to-cart rate by 18% by creating stronger urgency and perceived value for new visitors.
Rationale: New visitors often hesitate with generic “Shop Now” CTAs due to lack of immediate incentive. A discount-focused message like “Get 20% Off Your First Order” creates perceived value, lowers perceived risk, and builds urgency for first-time buyers, likely lifting add-to-cart rate among cold traffic.
Design Brief: www.fakeurl.com

Hypothesis: Replacing the sticky “Add to Cart” button with a floating one that includes a mini product image + “Only 3 left!” stock warning will boost mobile conversions by 22% by reducing friction and adding scarcity FOMO.
Rationale: Mobile users frequently abandon carts when the sticky Add to Cart button feels disconnected from the product. Adding a floating button with a mini product image keeps visual context, while the “Only 3 left!” scarcity trigger creates FOMO and urgency. This combo reduces decision friction and boosts impulse conversions on mobile.
Design Brief: www.fakeurl.com

Hypothesis: If we add a persistent sticky footer on mobile with the price and a CTA, then cart additions will increase because the "path to purchase" is always available during a long-form scroll.
Rationale: Heatmaps on long-scroll pages often show a "dead zone" in the middle where users are convinced but cannot find the button, leading to bounce.
Design Brief: A slim, bottom-anchored bar. Left side: Thumbnail of product + "£85 Upgrade". Right side: Purple button "Claim My Discount".
Development Brief: Trigger bar visibility only after the user scrolls 15% down the page. Ensure it does not overlap with Live Chat icons.

Hypothesis: If we change CTA copy to first-person ("I Want Triple Stimulation") instead of second-person ("Upgrade Your Pleasure"), then ATC will increase because it aligns with internal user desire.
Rationale: Psychological studies in CRO show that "I want..." statements reduce the "command" feel of a button and increase the user's sense of agency.
Design Brief: Design Brief: No layout change. Update text on all primary buttons to: "I Want The Upgrade" and "I Want Triple Stimulation".

Hypothesis: If we replace the small magnifying glass icon with a full-width search input field that includes placeholder text like "Search for a sensation (e.g., Suction)", then search usage and conversion will increase because users can find specific needs without digging through menus.
Rationale: Search users typically convert at 2-3x the rate of browse users. On mobile, the current search icon is a small target; making it an open field encourages high-intent "power users" to act.
Design Brief: Move search from a hidden icon to a permanent bar at the top of the mobile screen. Use a light grey background for the input and a clear placeholder: "Find your pleasure...".

Hypothesis: If we insert a CTA block after every primary feature description (Licking, Thrusting, Silent Tech), then the Add-to-Cart rate will increase by capturing impulse at the point of highest excitement.
Rationale: Currently, the page makes the user read the entire "story" before offering a button. This test treats each feature as a standalone reason to buy.
Design Brief: Simple, clean buttons placed directly beneath each feature paragraph. Text: "Get this Upgrade".

Hypothesis: If we add "Plain Packaging" and "Warranty" icons directly under every primary CTA button, then the CTR will increase because we are handling objections at the moment of peak friction.
Rationale: Users often hover over a button and think "Will my roommate see the box?" or "What if it breaks?". Answering these under the button prevents them from leaving to find the shipping policy.
Design Brief: Small, monochromatic icons (box, shield, truck) with 2-word labels placed 10px below the main buttons.

Hypothesis: If we add a short video or "sound bar" comparing a standard vibrator's noise to the Triple Vibe's silent motor, then CVR will increase among users living with roommates or partners.
Rationale: "How loud is it?" is a top-3 hidden objection. Proving the silence through a visual decibel meter or video demonstration removes that specific barrier by providing tangible proof.
Design Brief: A "Play Sound" button next to a decibel graphic showing the Triple Vibe operates at <40dB (whisper quiet). Use a wave-form animation to visualize the low frequency. *Second Variant with Comparison

Hypothesis: If we show a 5-second loop of a plain, unbranded brown box being delivered and opened to reveal the Triple Vibe, then Cart Abandonment will decrease.
Rationale: Users fear a giant "VIBRATOR" logo on their doorstep. Showing the literal brown box removes the "Delivery Anxiety" that kills sales at the final step.
Design Brief: A high-quality looping GIF or video. Frame 1: A plain brown box on a doorstep (no logos). Frame 2: Box opens to reveal a sleek, premium Cosara box inside. *Create a second variant where the GIF is placed in "Is your packaging discreet" question itself

Hypothesis: Replacing standard quantity selectors with pre-set boxes (1 Unit, 3 Units, 6 Units) will anchor the user toward higher-quantity purchases, significantly increasing AOV.
Rationale: Your founder identified that the current UI makes it hard to order more than 1 unit, and Ron noted that competing brands focus heavily on "packs".
Design Brief: Redesign the PDP buy box to feature three large, selectable blocks above the "Add to Cart" button. Block 1: "1 Pair", Block 2: "3 Pairs (Most Popular)", Block 3: "6 Pairs (Best Value)".

Hypothesis: Highlighting the "Top 5" best-selling variants (Navy, Black, Caribbean Blue, etc.) and hiding the rest behind a "See More" toggle will reduce choice paralysis and drive more conversions.
Rationale: The 70% of revenue comes from the "Core 3" colors. Simplifying the view prioritizes these winners.
Design Brief: Limit the initial variant grid to 5 thumbnails. Add a "+ More Colors" button that expands to show the full range.

Hypothesis: Adding a technical trust bar highlighting medical-grade quality will validate the premium price point for both healthcare pros and new demographics (travelers/athletes).
Rationale: Ron mentioned that their brand DNA is medical apparel. Highlighting "50k+ Nurses" and "12-hour comfort" anchors that quality.
Design Brief: Add a clean, 3-icon grid immediately below the "Add to Cart" button. Use thin, medical-style icons. Icons: 1. "Medical-Grade Compression", 2. "Trusted by 50k+ Professionals", 3. "12hr Shift Comfort".

Test Description: Quantity Reward Offer Test (Gift vs. Discount)
Hypothesis: Testing a "Buy 4, Get 1 Free" or "Free Shipping" offer against the standard % discount will prove a higher emotional value, leading to more multi-unit orders.
Rationale: Your founder suggested changing quantity break rewards from discounts to physical rewards or shipping benefits to test AOV impact.
Design Brief: Update the site-wide banner and the PDP quantity blocks to feature the "FREE" offer (e.g., "Add 3 Pairs for FREE Shipping").

Hypothesis: Forcing the first 4 slots on collection pages to be Bundles/Packs will anchor users at a higher price point, making single pairs look cheaper and bundles look like a better deal.
Rationale: Connor identified a huge opportunity to push "Black Socks Packs" directly rather than just single pairs.
Design Brief: Configure the product grid to inject the "Core 3-Pack" and "Bestseller 6-Pack" into the first four slots of the collection view.

Hypothesis: Adding a progress bar and "Quick Add" options for Navy, Black, and Caribbean Blue in the cart will nudge single-item buyers to complete a 3-pack set.
Rationale: The "Core 3" colors drive the majority of revenue. This cart logic leans into that existing behavior.
Design Brief: Inside the slide-out cart, add a visual bar that fills as users add Core 3 items. Below the bar, show a "Missing these?" section with quick-add buttons for the other core colors.

Hypothesis: Replacing the sticky “Add to Cart” button with a floating one that includes a mini product image + “Only 3 left!” stock warning will boost mobile conversions by 22% by reducing friction and adding scarcity FOMO.
Rationale: Mobile users frequently abandon carts when the sticky Add to Cart button feels disconnected from the product. Adding a floating button with a mini product image keeps visual context, while the “Only 3 left!” scarcity trigger creates FOMO and urgency. This combo reduces decision friction and boosts impulse conversions on mobile.
Design Brief: www.fakeurl.com

Hypothesis: Placing visual "proof" immediately after the claim will validate the product's efficacy before the user has a chance to bounce.
Rationale: Skincare users prioritize visual evidence over text. Moving B&As higher addresses the "does this actually work?" objection early in the scroll.
Design Brief: Insert a "Real Results" section with a high-contrast Before & After carousel. Use clear "Day 1" and "Day 28" labels. Keep the background clean and clinical to emphasize the skin changes.

Hypothesis: Grouping ingredients by "Ayurvedic" and "Clinical" satisfies both the holistic and results-oriented personas simultaneously.
Rationale: The current list is a "wall of text." A grid or table allows users to self-segment and find the proof points they care about most.
Placement: Middle of page, replacing the long vertical ingredient descriptions.
Design Brief: Design a 2-column or 4-box grid. Column 1: "The Ayurvedic Powerhouse" (Lotus/Lily icons). Column 2: "The Clinical Powerhouse" (Ceramide/Kelp icons). Use short, 2-line descriptions for each.

Hypothesis: Adding an authoritative expert voice will increase the "Believability" score of the anti-aging claims.
Rationale: Authority bias suggests users trust clinical endorsements more than peer reviews for technical skin concerns like "crepey skin."
Placement: Between the "How it Works" section and User Reviews.
Design Brief: Add a clean block with a professional headshot of an Ayurvedic Practitioner or Dermatologist. Include a quote about why the "String-to-Silk" technology is superior for the delicate neck barrier.

Test Description: "Is This Right For You?" Dosha Quiz CTA
Hypothesis: Using a quiz to "match" the user to the product increases psychological ownership and likelihood to buy.
Rationale: Micro-commitments (clicking through a quiz) lower the barrier to the final purchase.
Placement: Mid-page, after the initial product introduction.
Design Brief: A prominent, lifestyle-image-backed CTA card. Copy: "Find Your Ayurvedic Match. Take the 30-Second Skin Quiz." Buttons lead to a mini-quiz flow. Use shopify apps for reference.

Hypothesis: Seeing the unique texture in motion will better explain the "String-to-Silk" USP and drive higher intent.
Rationale: Sensory products (gels/masks) sell better when the user can visually "feel" the texture.
Placement: Hero section background or side media element.
Design Brief: Use a 5-second, high-quality loop of the gel stretching between fingers and then instantly melting into skin. Minimalist, no sound, high-res.

Hypothesis: Showing live activity creates a "crowded restaurant" effect, validating the brand for first-time visitors.
Rationale: Social proof is most effective when it feels current and "active."
Placement: Floating at the bottom corner of the screen.
Design Brief: Standard notification bubble. Small product image + "Recently Purchased: Maria in Vancouver just bought the 2-Bottle Bundle!" 10-second intervals. Use Shopify app for reference.

Hypothesis: Allowing users to choose their "lens" (Tradition or Science) will increase time on page and trust.
Rationale: Segmenting the pitch helps convert both the "naturalist" and the "skeptic."
Design Brief: A "pill-shaped" toggle button at the top of the info section. Left: "The Ayurvedic Story." Right: "The Clinical Proof." Content below changes based on selection.

Test Description: H1 Headline: Innovation vs. Direct Benefit
Hypothesis: Testing a "Results-First" headline will outperform the "Category-First" headline.
Rationale: Direct benefits usually convert better than "Innovation" for cold traffic.
Design Brief: Variant A: "The World's First String-to-Silk™ Neck Mask." Variant B: "Visibly Lift & Smooth Sagging Neck Skin in 4 Weeks."

Hypothesis: Replacing static text with a visual progress bar that updates toward the $28 free shipping threshold will increase AOV.
Rationale: The current cart uses static text indicating free shipping above $28. A dynamic bar gamifies the experience and nudges users to add small items.
Placement: Top of the Cart page, above product list.
Design Brief: Horizontal bar at the top of the cart container. Copy: "You're only $[Amount] away from FREE Shipping!". Add Copy for all stages (You've unlocked FREE SHIPPING!)

Hypothesis: Reducing the carousel from 20+ options to 3 static, high-converting "Essentials" will increase conversion by eliminating decision fatigue.
Rationale: Excessive choice leads to analysis paralysis. Presenting three clear, distinct options makes the choice effortless.
Placement: Replace the "You may also like" auto-carousel at the bottom of the cart.
Design Brief: Remove the carousel. Display exactly three static product cards side-by-side. Shorten titles for perfect alignment. Use best sellers (With different price points)

Hypothesis: Swapping the order of "Shop by Category" and "Shop by Concern" will increase CTR to product pages.
Rationale: Users typically shop by a skin problem (e.g., "Crepey Skin" or "Dryness") rather than a product format. Highlighting "Concern" first aligns with user intent.
Design Brief: In the "SHOP" drop-down, move "SHOP BY CONCERN" to the first (left-most) position. Move "SHOP BY CATEGORY" to the second position.

Hypothesis: Replacing a low-utility "Order Notes" field with a discounted "Mini" product nudge will UPT and AOV.
Rationale: Order notes are rarely used. This high-value real estate is better used for a discounted point-of-purchase impulse buy.
Placement: Replacing the "Order Notes" text box in the cart.
Design Brief: Remove the "Order Notes" box. Insert a "One-Time Offer" box: "Add a Mini Best-Seller for 20% OFF?" (e.g., Mini Blavana or Yauvari) with a one-click "Add" button.

Hypothesis: Organizing bestsellers by skin concern rather than a generic list will increase relevance and click-through.
Rationale: Organizing by problem (e.g., "Top for Aging Skin," "Top for Dryness") allows users to self-segment and find their match instantly on the homepage.
Design Brief: Create a tabbed section titled "Top Solutions for Your Skin." Rows: "For Aging & Fine Lines," "For Dryness & Glow," "For Sagging & Crepey Skin".

Test Description: "Love the Taste or It's Free" Guarantee
Hypothesis: If we add a specific "Taste-Match" badge near the ATC, then CVR will increase by addressing the primary barrier to protein purchases: taste anxiety.
Rationale: Users hesitate to buy large bags of new flavors. A specific taste guarantee removes the psychological risk of being stuck with a chalky product.
Design Brief: Small, high-contrast badge placed 10px below or beside the ATC button: "100% Taste Guarantee: Love it or it's Free."

Hypothesis: If we add "Order in X hours to receive in [State] by [Date]," then CVR will increase by creating urgency and removing logistics anxiety.
Rationale: Knowing exactly when a replenishment product will arrive is a massive conversion lever for users running low on supply.
Design Brief: Clean, sans-serif text with a small truck icon. Dynamic date highlighted in bold or brand color. Use Shop Promise for reference.

Hypothesis: If we move the "One-Time Purchase" option to a text link below the main Add to Cart button, then Subscription CVR will increase by making subscription the default path.
Rationale: High-growth brands use this to prioritize recurring revenue. It frames the subscription as the "standard" way to buy while keeping the price anchor high.
Design Brief: Default the subscription radio button to "on." Style the One-Time Purchase as a secondary, underlined text link beneath the primary ATC button.

Hypothesis: If we add a toggle in the cart drawer to "Switch to Subscribe & Save," then Subscription CVR will increase by capturing users swayed by final price savings.
Rationale: Seeing the total price in the cart is the "moment of truth." A 20% discount here is a powerful final nudge.
Design Brief: Add a "Save$X  instantly" toggle under the item in the cart drawer. Use a distinct brand green for the savings highlight.
Development Brief: AJAX cart update to swap One-Time item for Subscription variant without page refresh.

Hypothesis: If we replace the static quantity selectors with a "Build a Bundle" experience and a dynamic gamification bar that unlocks tiered discounts (10% off at 3 bags, 15% off at 4+ bags) in addition to free shipping, then AOV and CVR will increase by incentivizing bulk purchases through a clear value exchange.
Rationale: The current buybox lacks a clear "reward" for adding more bags beyond the $50 shipping threshold. By introducing a progress bar that visualizes "Free Shipping" at 2 bags and subsequent tiered discounts, we leverage loss aversion to push users toward higher basket sizes.
Design Brief: * Progress Bar: Place a high-contrast progress bar directly above the flavor grid.  Milestones: Mark clear milestones on the bar:  2 Bags: "FREE SHIPPING UNLOCKED".  3 Bags: "ADD 1 MORE FOR 10% OFF".  4+ Bags: "BEST VALUE: 15% OFF".  Bundle Summary: Add a dynamic text line above the ATC button that reflects the current savings (e.g., "You're saving $12.00 on this bundle!").

Test Description: Sitewide Progress Bar: Tiered Reward Incentive
Hypothesis: If we implement a new dynamic announcement bar that tracks progress toward Free Shipping and a Free Shaker, then AOV and CVR will increase by creating a persistent secondary goal for the user throughout their entire browsing journey.
Rationale: Without an announcement bar, users may not realize they are close to a value threshold (like Free Shipping at 2 bags) until they reach the cart or checkout. By introducing a dynamic bar that visualizes progress toward $50 (Free Shipping) and $120 (Free Shaker), we gamify the site experience and incentivize users to add higher quantities to their cart to "win" the rewards.
Design Brief: Inject New Component: Add a thin bar at the very top of the site (above the navigation).  Dynamic Text: Use a string that updates based on cart value: "Add $[X] for FREE Shipping" → "Add $[X] for a FREE Shaker."  Visual Progress: Include a thin brand-green progress line that fills as the cart value grows.  Reward 1 ($50): "FREE Shipping Unlocked! 🚚 Add $[X] more for a FREE Shaker!"  Reward 2 ($120): "CONGRATS! Free Shaker + Shipping Unlocked! 🎉"

Test Description: Price Per Serving & Total Savings Visualization
Hypothesis: If we emphasize the "$2.66/serving" cost and show "You Save $10.00" in high-contrast green, then SCVR will increase by making the value undeniable.
Rationale: Breaking down large lump sums into a "Daily Ritual" cost triggers rational value logic, making the purchase feel like a smaller daily investment.
Design Brief: Use bolded green text for "$2.66 / SERVING" and "SAVE 20% TODAY" next to the subscription radio button. Ensure the font size is larger than the standard price.

Hypothesis: If we add a "Frequently Bought Together" section in the cart drawer with low-friction items, then AOV will increase via impulse purchases.
Rationale: Once a user commits to a $50 bag, adding a $10 shaker or smaller accessory feels like a minor marginal cost that doesn't require heavy research.
Design Brief: "Add X more and get X (Free Shipping)" header at the bottom of the drawer. 2–3 horizontal product cards with "Quick Add" (+) buttons for easy interaction. If above 2 products already, show "Complete your Routine"

Hypothesis: If we implement a video testimonial carousel specifically showcasing people drinking the shake, then CVR will increase by removing "Taste Anxiety" at the point of purchase.
Rationale: Users at the bottom of the funnel are often hesitant about texture and flavor. Seeing authentic video reviews of customers enjoying the taste provides the final nudge needed to "Add to Cart."
Design Brief: Horizontal scroller of 9:16 vertical videos under buybox/ATC.
